title: Warning kind 'settings-file-malformed' diverges from declared type 'settings-parse-error'
description: outputStyle.ts emits a warning with kind 'settings-file-malformed' on the JSON.parse failure branch, but warnings.ts declares only 'settings-parse-error' (line 44-47) for that condition. The OutputStyleWarning interface in outputStyle.ts uses a loose 'kind: string', so TypeScript does not catch the mismatch, but any consumer that narrows on the EngineWarning discriminated union (warnings.ts:139-148) will fail to match this kind — the warning escapes typed narrowing and silently degrades to the unknown bucket.
impact: Downstream warning consumers (start.ts pushes it onto warnings array; LLM-facing renderers narrow on the union) cannot identify or handle the malformed-settings case. A real parse failure of ~/.claude/settings.json produces a warning that no typed handler will pattern-match, so the user-facing remediation step is lost. This is the same class of silent-failure the H002 fix was meant to close.
recommendation: Either rename the emitted kind to 'settings-parse-error' to match the declared type, or add 'settings-file-malformed' to the SettingsParseErrorWarning union in warnings.ts. Prefer the rename — it is the smaller diff and the existing declared type is referenced from EngineWarning.
evidence:
kind: 'settings-file-malformed',
        message: `Failed to parse ${settingsPath}: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`,

title: selectPath corrupt-without-archivedTo path leaves user wedged with no recovery guidance
description: When loadState returns kind='corrupt' AND archivedTo is undefined (archive write failed — ENOSPC, EACCES on .sui-deepbook-course, etc.), selectPath returns `{ ok: false, errors: ['State corrupt: <message>'] }`. The user is now wedged: state.json is corrupt, no archive was written, and the error string only reflects loadState's diagnostic (e.g. 'archive write also failed (ENOSPC)') without telling the user what manual remediation step would unblock them (e.g. 'remove .sui-deepbook-course/state.json by hand and re-run /start').
impact: On a low-disk or restricted-permission environment, the user sees an opaque error and has no path forward. This is the precise failure mode the H003 cycle was meant to close for the 'archive succeeded' branch but it remains open for the 'archive failed' branch — and that branch is the harder-to-reach one in tests, so it ships untested in the recovery direction.
recommendation: Append a manual-remediation hint to the errors array: e.g. `'State corrupt and archive write failed; manually delete <statePath> and re-run /start. Original error: ${stateResult.message}'`. No code-path change needed — only the error string. Cheap, high-leverage.

title: findExistingArchive silent skip on per-archive read failure can produce duplicate archives
description: Inside the for-loop, `catch (_readErr)` swallows per-archive read errors and continues. If a previously-written archive happens to be unreadable for the current process (EACCES because someone tightened perms, or partial-write race during prior archiveCorruptFile call), findExistingArchive returns null, and archiveCorruptFile then writes a fresh archive — defeating the H003 dedup guarantee for AC-7.2 (count of state.corrupt-*.json files MUST equal 1, not N).
impact: Edge case but real: a corrupt-state recovery on a directory whose prior archive lost its read perms will silently double-archive. Users in long-lived environments accumulate archives; AC-7.2's invariant 'count == 1' breaks. The error is invisible — there is no log, warning, or counter.
recommendation: On per-archive readFile failure, surface the skip via a structured trace (return-value annotation or a `warnings` accumulator). At minimum, distinguish ENOENT (race: file deleted between readdir and readFile, benign) from EACCES (permissions, alarming). Treat EACCES on an existing archive as a signal that dedup is unreliable; either propagate as a fatal error or include the unreadable archive's name in the LoadStateResult message.

title: archiveCorruptFile dedup has TOCTOU race between findExistingArchive and writeFile
description: Two concurrent /start invocations against the same corrupt state.json both call findExistingArchive (line 156), both observe no existing archive, both increment archiveCounter (line 165) to different values, both proceed to fsPromises.writeFile with the wx flag. Because each invocation generates a unique filename via the timestamp+counter suffix, the wx flag never triggers a collision — both writes succeed, leaving 2 archives with identical bytes. archiveCounter is per-process, so two processes (e.g. an IDE and a CLI invoking the MCP server simultaneously) would also race regardless.
impact: AC-7.2's archive de-duplication invariant ('exactly 1 archive per unique corrupt content') breaks under concurrent /start. The wx flag protects only against identical-name collisions; content-based dedup with no lock is racy by construction. In practice the user-visible impact is minor (a second archive of the same bytes is harmless leak), but the contract-stated invariant is violated.
recommendation: Use the content hash as part of the archive filename (e.g. `state.corrupt-${ts}-${hash}.json`), so wx-flag protection becomes content-aware. Two concurrent writes with identical content collide on filename → wx fails → second writer detects and resolves to the existing archive. Combined with the existing findExistingArchive scan, this closes the race deterministically without lockfiles.

title: cycle-e2e-pass.sh skip and pass share exit code 0; CI cannot distinguish from $?
description: Both PASSED and SKIPPED outcomes exit 0. A naive CI step that only inspects $? sees green for both, masking the fact that E-008 was never actually exercised. The header documents this behavior ('all processed scenarios passed OR were skipped with a reason → 0'), but downstream automation rarely parses stdout for `SKIPPED:` prefixes — it greps exit code. AC-5 specifies the structured stdout record but not an exit-code distinction.
impact: A CI pipeline that runs cycle-e2e-pass.sh on every PR will report 'all green' even when the host's Sui CLI is permanently out of range, hiding regression risk for E-008. False sense of coverage. The risk is silent: nothing in the pipeline tells the operator that a scenario has been silently skipped for weeks.
recommendation: Either (a) emit a non-zero-but-non-failure code (e.g. 77, the autotools convention for 'skipped'), or (b) write a machine-readable JSON receipt to a known file (e.g. .forge/e2e/last-run.json) listing each scenario's outcome with status='passed'|'skipped'|'failed' and a reason. Option (b) is preferred because it survives shell exit-code conflation and is greppable by CI. Add a final line summarizing: `echo "E2E_RUN: passed=N skipped=M failed=K"` so humans can spot skips at a glance.
evidence:
# Exit codes:
#   0 — all processed scenarios passed or were skipped with a reason
#   1 — one or more scenarios failed without a skip reason
# ...
emit_skip "E-008" "sui-cli out of supported range..."
    return 0

title: cycle-e2e-pass.sh unknown-scenario fallthrough silently exits 0
description: The case dispatch's `*)` arm prints `INFO: scenario=${SCENARIO_FILTER} not handled by this script` and exits 0. A typo in the scenario id (e.g. `E-08` instead of `E-008`) produces a green run that exercised nothing. Combined with R3-005's exit-code conflation, this is a silent-no-op pattern.
impact: An operator running `cycle-e2e-pass.sh E-009` (a scenario this script does not yet handle) sees INFO + exit 0 and assumes coverage. As more scenarios are added to scenarios.json, the gap between 'scenarios this script handles' and 'scenarios in scenarios.json' will grow invisibly.
recommendation: Treat unknown scenario filter as an error: emit `ERROR: scenario=<id> not handled by this script (handled scenarios: E-008)` and exit 2. Reserve exit 1 for genuine scenario failures. If the empty-string filter is meant to mean 'run all', keep that branch as-is but require any explicit filter to match a known scenario.
evidence:
*)
    echo "INFO: scenario=${SCENARIO_FILTER} not handled by this script"
    ;;
esac

exit 0

title: sui_cli_in_range conflates 'sui not installed' with 'sui out of range' in skip message
description: When `sui` is not on PATH at all, `sui_version_string` returns an empty string (because of `2>/dev/null || echo ""`). Then sui_cli_in_range parses empty as out-of-range, and the skip message becomes `sui-cli out of supported range (got: ; supported: 1.63.2-1.64.1)`. The user cannot distinguish 'sui CLI not installed' from 'sui CLI version 1.69 (genuinely out of range)'.
impact: Diagnostic clarity issue, not a correctness issue. A user who has not installed Sui sees a misleading 'out of range' skip message when the real problem is a missing binary. They may waste time downgrading a non-existent install.
recommendation: Distinguish the two cases in run_e008: check `command -v sui` first; if absent, emit `SKIPPED: scenario=E-008 reason='sui CLI not installed (required: 1.63.2-1.64.1)'`. Only fall through to the range check when the binary exists.

title: outputStyle.ts: enabledPlugins=[] (array) silently treated as 'plugin not enabled' rather than 'malformed settings'
description: When enabledPlugins is a JSON array (e.g. `"enabledPlugins": []`), the typeof-object check passes (typeof [] === 'object') and the null check passes ([] !== null). The code then casts to Record<string, unknown> and reads PLUGIN_KEY, which returns undefined. The function falls through to the 'pluginEnabled !== true' branch and returns the same 'output-style-plugin-not-enabled' warning as the legitimate 'plugin key absent from object' case. The user sees a misleading 'enable the plugin' message when the actual problem is a malformed settings file.
impact: Diagnostic accuracy: the user gets the wrong remediation hint. They will dutifully run `claude plugins enable learning-output-style@claude-plugins-official` and the issue will persist because settings.json is structurally malformed (an array where an object was expected).
recommendation: Add an `Array.isArray(parsed.enabledPlugins)` check alongside the typeof/null checks. If it is an array, emit a 'settings-file-malformed' (or 'settings-parse-error', see R3-001) warning instead of 'plugin-not-enabled'. This shifts the user toward fixing settings.json rather than re-enabling a plugin.
